blocks.text: Praise the Lord! Sing to the Lord a new song, his praise in the assembly of the faithful.

Ps 149: Praise for Israel’s task in the world. This fourth hymn in the series of five concluding the Psalms may disturb readers by its apparent call to vengeance against foreigners. Ps 149 develops the ideas of two psalms, the immediately preceding Ps 148, which singles out Israel as having a mission to proclaim the supremacy of the to the nations (Ps 148.14), and Ps 2 (second from the beginning as Ps 149 is second from the last) in which the Davidic king is given authority to subdue the nations. Ps 149 transposes the royal role of Ps 2 to the entire nation (as in Isa 55.3). Ps 149 has two stanzas, vv. 1–4 and 5–9, the first inviting praise for the ’s victory and the second equating that praise metaphorically with weapons of war against the rebellious kings.

1 : , as in Isa 42.10 and other psalms (e.g., 33.3; 96.1; 98.1); here in response to creation, for Israel is (v. 1a); the act establishes the kingship of the . In the previous psalm, God as king is creating the universe, while here he creates Israel.
Let Israel be glad in its Maker; let the children of Zion rejoice in their King.
Let them praise his name with dancing, making melody to him with tambourine and lyre.
For the Lord takes pleasure in his people; he adorns the humble with victory.
Let the faithful exult in glory; let them sing for joy on their couches.
5 : , symbolizes private (in contrast to public) expression as in Pss 4.4; 6.6; 36.4; Hos 7.14.
Let the high praises of God be in their throats and two‐edged swords in their hands,

6 : Wielding a (and the other military actions in vv. 7–9) develops a traditional theme of the Israelite king and the people representing the ’s kingship over the world, which often meant conflict with the nations (cf. Pss 2; 46; 48). Here that tradition can be understood as a metaphor for proclaiming the ’s sovereignty over kings and nations by Israel’s faithful worship of the alone. The conjunction seems to make the statements of v. 6a and 6b parallel: praising God is like wielding a sword. Israel has the task of embodying divine sovereignty and rendering it visible in the world. This unmasks false gods and invites the nations to join in God’s praise and recognition of Israel.
to execute vengeance on the nations and punishment on the peoples,
7 : , best rendered “retribution” for the kings’ refusal to recognize their true suzerain.
to bind their kings with fetters and their nobles with chains of iron,
to execute on them the judgment decreed. This is glory for all his faithful ones. Praise the Lord!
